html:
Josefina of Leuchtenberg, born Joséphine Maximilienne Eugénie Napoléone de Beauharnais on March 14, 1807, in Milan, and died on June 7, 1876, in Stockholm, was the Queen of Sweden and Norway from 1844 to 1859, as the wife of King Oscar I. Her father was the French general Eugène de Beauharnais, stepson and adopted son of Napoleon I. Her mother was Princess Augusta of Bavaria, daughter of King Maximilian I of Bavaria. Josefina was a princess of Bologna from birth, and from 1813, Duchess of Galliera in her own right.

== Biography ==

=== Childhood === Josefina was born on March 14, 1807, at the viceroy's residence in Milan. She was the eldest child of the Viceroy of Italy, Eugène de Beauharnais, who had been appointed by Napoleon in 1805. At her baptism, she received the names Joséphine Maximilienne Eugénie Napoléonne; the first name was at the request of Napoleon, and the last as a tribute to him. Just a few months after her birth, on December 29th, she was appointed Princess of Bologna, and on May 28, 1813, she was named Duchess of Galliera. During her childhood, she lived in the castle in Monza, near Milan. After Napoleon's fall in 1814, Eugène de Beauharnais attempted to declare himself King of Italy, but when this failed, the family was forced to flee to Munich. In 1817, he bought the city and principality of Eichstätt, where the family had a residence, and was appointed Duke of Leuchtenberg; the family lived in Munich during the winter, at the castle in Ismaning during the summer, and in Eichstätt during the autumn. Josefina's native language was French, and she and her siblings also learned German, Italian, and English. Other subjects included arithmetic, history, geography, and astronomy. On March 24, 1821, Josefina was confirmed by a Roman Catholic priest. As the eldest sibling, she also took care of her younger siblings. She has been described as a beloved and cheerful child by the German philosopher Friedrich von Schelling, who worked in Munich.

=== Wedding === From 1821 onwards, the Swedish King Charles XIV John was seriously focused on arranging the marriage of Crown Prince Oscar. There were several princesses of suitable age that the king considered particularly interesting: "You know my wishes; I want you to primarily focus on the young princess of Denmark, if she appeals to you and shares your feelings. Next, consider the princess of Leuchtenberg, then the one in Kassel, and finally, the one in Weimar," the king wrote in a letter to his son. In May 1822, Crown Prince Oscar embarked on a tour of Europe to meet these princesses. In Copenhagen, he met Princesses Caroline and Wilhelmina. In the Netherlands, he visited the royal court and was charmed by Princess Marianne, but her age, 12 years old, made marriage impossible.

On August 23rd, Oscar visited Eichstätt, where he met Josephine, who was then 15 years old, and her siblings. Before the Crown Prince's arrival, Josephine's father, Prince Eugène, had walked with her in the park and explained the purpose of the Crown Prince's visit. The Crown Prince was captivated by Josephine, and on August 26th, he formally proposed to her. Her being a Catholic was a topic of discussion before the wedding. Josephine was religious, while her father was religiously indifferent. However, the father requested that the Swedish Crown Prince, as a courtesy to Josephine's mother, Augusta, allow Josephine to maintain her faith. The Swedish advisors Wetterstedt and Löwenhielm had different opinions on what would be appropriate. However, there was a precedent: Queen Desideria had been allowed to retain her Catholic faith when she became the Swedish queen. In the marriage contract, it was stipulated that Josephine would participate in religious ceremonies.

Crown Prince Oscar then returned to Sweden, and Josephine began learning Swedish. When Count Wetterstedt returned to Munich in February 1823, he noted that she had made significant progress in the language: "I have already had the honor of conversing with Her Royal Highness in Swedish for more than half an hour."

The Catholic wedding ceremony took place in Munich on May 22, 1823, without the presence of the Crown Prince. Josefina was escorted to the altar by her father, Eugène, while Crown Prince Oscar was represented by Josefina's uncle, Karl of Bavaria. Two days later, Josefina left home with Countess Marcelle Tascher de la Pagerie, Baroness Wurms, and her lady-in-waiting, Berta Zück, to travel to Sweden. In Lübeck, they were met by Mariana Koskull and Countess Brahe and boarded the warship Carl XIII. Also on the journey to Sweden was Queen Desideria of Sweden, who had been absent from Sweden for 12 years. At Vaxholm, Crown Prince Oscar joined them, and when the ship arrived in Stockholm on June 13, 1823, tens of thousands of people had gathered on the shores.

Upon arrival in Stockholm, Josefina and the Queen boarded the sloop Vasaorden and disembarked at Manilla on the southern part of Djurgården island. The King and Crown Prince were waiting for them on the dock. The women then traveled by a grand carriage drawn by eight white horses to Haga Palace, where they were welcomed by the Princess.

How certain are we about this information?

People from the 19th century are very well documented (through census records, newspapers, and letters). The uncertainty mainly concerns selection and perspective – whose stories are highlighted.
tldr: Josefina of Leuchtenberg was a German-French princess who became Queen of Sweden and Norway after marrying King Oscar I in 1844.
lattlast:
Josefina was born in Italy in 1807. She grew up in a large family where she learned several languages, including French, German, and Italian. Her father was a successful man who bestowed the title of princess upon her.

When the Swedish Crown Prince Oscar was looking for a wife, he became very fond of Josefina. He met her in Germany in 1822 and asked for her hand in marriage. Before the wedding, it was important to determine what would happen regarding her religion, as she was Catholic while the Swedish monarchs were Protestants. It was decided that she could retain her faith.

Before the marriage, Josefina moved to Sweden to learn the language and became proficient in Swedish. She married in Munich in 1823 and then came to Stockholm, where she received a grand welcome from thousands of people. She served as Queen of Sweden until her death in 1876.
